behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is between ingestion/parsing and correlation modules: entries from different services must still land in the same trace bucket. A pure unit test of one grouping function can miss handoff bugs (e.g., service-specific field mapping or normalization differences). Integration-brake check: no real OS/network/subprocess required (so not System); no AI/ML output dependency (not Agentic); no multi-agent chain (not Workflow). Rejection-gate: rejected System because behavior text only requires grouping correctness across microservice-emitted records, not real network/process behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Wire parser + correlator + in-memory store/reporter, feed mixed records from multiple service names sharing trace IDs, and assert one merged group per trace ID with all entries present.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: The likely real failure is at module boundaries: timestamp parsing/normalization plus timeline assembly/render ordering policy. A single sort function unit test may miss parse-to-order contract issues across modules. Integration-brake check: no requirement for real OS/network/subprocess; no AI/ML dependence; no multi-step agent chain. Rejection-gate: rejected System because behavior text specifies timestamp/clock-skew ordering logic, not real infrastructure effects.
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end timeline assembly in-process with logs from multiple services containing controlled ±500ms skew, then assert resulting timeline order follows the skew policy and remains stable around boundary cases.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is usually in wiring baseline computation, percentile aggregation, threshold comparison, and reporting. Unit-testing percentile math alone can miss incorrect propagation into anomaly flags. Integration-brake check: no real OS/network/subprocess required; deterministic analytics (not Agentic); no multi-agent chain. Rejection-gate: rejected Agentic/System because behavior text is deterministic statistical detection and report flagging.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a controlled latency dataset through baseline calculator + p99 aggregator + anomaly reporter, including one window with p99 >3x baseline, and assert exactly that window is flagged in the generated anomaly report.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: This is pipeline resilience across parser, error handling, and metrics/reporting modules. Unit parser tests alone can miss failures where parse errors still crash downstream stages or skipped-count is not propagated. Integration-brake check: no explicit real OS/network/subprocess dependency; no AI/ML dependence; no multi-agent sequence. Rejection-gate: rejected System because behavior text focuses on in-pipeline handling/reporting semantics, not real infrastructure behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full in-process ingestion pipeline with a mixed stream of valid and malformed JSON lines, then assert process continuity, valid records are still processed, and skipped-entry count matches malformed input count.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real throughput/backpressure behavior under load (scheduler timing, queue pressure, runtime I/O characteristics). In-process mocked timing often hides drops/lag that only appear with real process/runtime conditions. Not Agentic/Workflow because no AI/ML judgment or multi-agent chain is involved. Rejection-gate: rejected Integration because behavior text explicitly requires burst-rate handling ('10,000 log entries per second') and no-drop/no-lag guarantees, which are infrastructure-sensitive.
behaviors.test_description: Run the actual tool process under a real load generator producing 10k entries/sec for a sustained window, then verify ingested count equals sent count, processing latency stays bounded, and backlog does not grow unbounded during/after the burst.
